speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us more about your professional life and how it's finished up for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I spent many years with the firm of-- the American firm of Management Consultants. And I worked, as I mentioned before, in Sweden, in America, in Canada, quite some period in Germany, and of course here in the United Kingdom. And then in 1980, unfortunately, I was coming home by car from London where I had visited with my mother, who by that time was living in an old-age home, and we were involved in a horrific car crash. All my three children were in the car, but thank god their injuries were fundamentally minor, if at all, by comparison with what happened to me. I was rather badly knocked about. And I was in an intensive care unit in a hospital for a number of weeks. And then, sum total, spent about 33 weeks in hospital and then ultimately graduated to a wheelchair and onto crutches. And ultimately, with the help of some outstanding medical treatment, all under the National Health Service here that extended over five years, I am reasonably fit again today. I don't need crutches. And I lead a very normal life. I don't suffer to any degree anymore from any of my injuries. I can't walk as fast or play football, nor can I walk for any lengthy distances, but you know, bearing in mind my age, I'm not in any way complaining. But obviously, that meant an end to my career as a management consultant, which required a considerable amount of mobility, not only within the United Kingdom, but also within Europe and the Americas. And so I was invited one day by the local university to address some final year students on management, in general terms, and that went down reasonably well. And I was asked to give another talk like that. And then they offered me a position as a part-time tutor. And that developed into a part-time lecturer and into a full-time, into a full-time lecture on a special contractual sort of basis. And I was greatly encouraged to be asked to found and start a special course, a one-year diploma course in European Management Studies. And that consisted only of European students, of Germans, Spaniards, Italians, French, Greeks, and so on. And I ran this for about five years, until in fact, I reached the tender age now of 70, when I thought it was high time that I ought to make room for some younger people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, although in my own professional area, I didn't work anymore as a consultant, I obviously managed to make a contribution to the university, or certainly to the students of the university, by developing courses based to a very large extent on my experience and to specific areas. One area, which is referred to as operations management, which deals in broad terms with the organizational factories, and the people involved in that. And the other one in human resource development, which fundamentally deals with the behavior of human beings as individuals and in groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's an area in management which I feel is far too much neglected. We tend to get ourselves involved in all sorts of wonderful systems and concepts, but in the end-- in outstanding technological advancement-- but in the end, let's face it, that however sophisticated your equipment is, computers and everything else, you need monkeys like your good self and my good self to press the buttons, to monitor the progress of those particular machines, and to remain totally and completely in charge of operations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you've told us now about your career as a practical engineer, and a process engineer, management consultant, and then in academic life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this different from how your--how different is it from how your life would have panned out had you remained in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ooh, I think totally different. I think I mentioned at the beginning that I was destined to go into my grandfather's and to my father's business. And I don't know, I was never, I never had a particular liking for business activities. I still don't like it today. And I don't know. I suppose I could have had the option of rebelling against the family wishes and say it's not for me, and find somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is possible that I might have gotten used to it under duress? It's even possible that I might have been successful. Who knows? But if it were to go according to my particular free will, I don't think I would have changed that. I do not like business. And I'll tell you why.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in many cases, human relations, which to my mind are vital, and absolutely paramount in any activities-- be it political, be it social, be it academic, be it business-- tend to go sour. Or too many of them-- and I'm referring to human relations-- tend to go sour by virtue of some pressures or temptations or whatever else it may be within the business world. And that's something that I would certainly not like, and certainly would not appreciate.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the subject of human relationships--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us how you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I met this charming girl who now happens to be my wife. And she's been my wife for the last 32 or 33 years, purely and simply because she came from South Africa. And she, together with two of her friends, went on arrival in the United Kingdom to the Edinburgh Music Festivals. And she met three other guys who also happened to be Jewish, who offered them a lift on one particular occasion. And so the six of them teamed up, as it were, and spent the rest of their holidays in Edinburgh around this cultural activity, this international cultural activity. And then they came to London. It was through one of those people, one of those three boys or men who were out there, who offered a lift to the three girls, was my close friend, again. Herman, who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Herman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirschberger, who I had mentioned early on in our talk. And it was through him that I was introduced to who is now my wife, a charming girl in those days, and a charming wife these days. And we've been happily married now for 32, 33 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have three children. We have one daughter and we have two sons. And they, well, they're pursuing their own development and careers. And they use every possible opportunity to come home here, because this is their base. And so we're happy to say-- I'm happy to say, at least-- we're a very happy family and a very close knit family. And fortunately, a very Jewish family, insofar that we don't rush to the synagogue every Shabbat, but we are all, including the children, very Jewish conscious. And I think, looking back on my life developments, that is very important, both to my wife and to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, looking back on the Holocaust, has it affected your family and your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my immediate family, of course, fortunately, they were not affected by it, insofar that my wife lived in South Africa. And when her father volunteered for service with the South African Forces, and was therefore part and parcel of the Eighth Army at one stage, they were reasonably well protected in South Africa. Protected, that means they were miles away from all the terrible things that happened, both as a war and as the atrocities.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, yes, they know quite a lot about it, because occasionally they listen to what I have to tell them about my experiences. And I hope this tape, perhaps, will also help a little bit in bringing one or two other things closer to their mind and to their children. After all, that is the purpose of all this. But how has it affected me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, while I lament, of course, the loss of certainly my grandfather, to whom I was very, very close-- he was a wonderful man-- and I lament the losses of aunts and uncles and cousins as well, I was, after all, only 12 years old when I left them all. And so I have now obviously grown used to living without them.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is painful, and what has perhaps had a very strong effect on me, was the experience on the Continent, in France and in Belgium, when I saw these desperately miserable survivors of humanity trudging down the road, not knowing will they get to Palestine? Will they survive the trip? Will they ever manage it? Will they be happy there? Will they ever be able to lead a normal life? That affected me quite considerably.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I try in my own little way to help as much as I can to alleviate human suffering. Whether that is by Jewish people or not Jewish people is totally immaterial. To me, they're all human beings. And I make very little difference between either religion or nationality, provided, of course, they're decent individuals to begin with. I think when we look at the problem, Jews vis-a-vis Germany, I think we've got to be very careful. It is, perhaps, very easy to condemn the entire German nation for the terrible atrocities that took place. I don't think for a moment we should ever condone this, we should ever forget it. But at the same time, I think we've got to remain conscious of two things, that amongst them were quite a lot of people who showed their humanity, even only in small ways, and thereby proved that they would be acceptable, I view, to any decent sort of thinking individual. I'm thinking, perhaps, of Mr. Sacher, the housekeeper. I'm thinking, perhaps, of the lady who came to clean and gained nothing, nothing personal. They only risked-- they only risked their own lives, or their own freedom, basically, by doing these sort of things. And I think they did this, purely and simply, because they were human beings as well. And whatever the mechanism tried to impose upon them was nevertheless something that needs to be considered. I met a lot of the Germans while I was working in Germany. And slowly but surely, I began to establish some kind of a mutual trust between myself and those individuals, some who'd been in the Nazi forces, some who, of course, were born either during the war or after the war, and obviously could not in any way be held responsible for this. And I transfer that concept, in my views, towards the Israel Palestinian conflict, because we have something very similar there. And until, in fact, we try to begin to understand the Palestinians in their own way are human beings and have a right to an existence in the same way as we claim to have, and we certainly do have, there must be some accommodation. And this can only be built up, in my view, by personal contacts. And of course, we've seen that in the past, that Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, the late Yitzhak Rabin, Paris, have obviously made considerable personal contact. And fortunately, that is now being taken up also by the presence of Prime Minister Netanyahu. But it is, this has got to be continued, and this has got to be extended. And it's not only just between the politicians, but it's also between the ordinary people. And where you have that sort of contact, of that facility of ordinary people, it is amazing how happy-- let me correct this-- how peacefully and how tolerantly they can, in fact, live next to each other. And I see this also at our university right now where we have 102 different nationalities. And they all get on like a house on fire. There are, of course, some political activists. But by and large, Israeli students and Palestinian students, they're all in the same boat. They're all over here and they're studying, and perhaps the common language is perhaps English rather than perhaps Hebrew or Arabic. Although many of those on both sides are bilingual by now. But that is our only hope, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is that your message, perhaps, that you'd like to record on this video for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think, personally, based on my own personal experience and my thinking on the basis of that, is that ordinary people-- forgetting politicians right now who most probably have rather different motivations-- but ordinary people can get on with each other far, far better if they're afforded the opportunity of meeting each other, perhaps on mutual ground to begin with, and establishing-- and that is, you know, everybody can establish that for him or herself straightaway-- that all human beings want to be left alone, want to be able to get on with their own work, want to raise their own families, and want to provide for their families. And that applies to people whether they're black or white or gypsies or Jews or anybody else. Yes, I think this is a very vital message that, if I may, would just like to underline here on the conclusion of this interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much indeed, Steven. And this is the end of Tape 6, and the end of our interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Cheer up.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a continuation of Tape 6. And Steven, I'm going to ask you to introduce your wife Hilary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This charming lady here is my wife of many years standing. It's 32 years now. And we've gone through thick and thin, but on balance, quite clearly the thick has come out very much from the top. And you might perhaps just like to add one or two impressions of your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband Steven, and I've actually been married to him for 34 years, although he hasn't been married to me for that long. Steve is actually a very exceptional person. He has the milk of human kindness flowing through his veins. And I am always astounded at his attitude of forgiveness towards a nation that committed their heinous crimes against personally, his family, and against the Jewish people and against humanity, in general. I was extremely fortunate in that I grew up in South Africa. I was born in 1937, so I knew very little about the war. We were extremely protected, amazingly protected in that growing up as children, I virtually knew nothing about the war. I only came to the experience of the Holocaust through meeting my husband when I was 24. I cannot believe how ignorant I was prior to that. But having heard the family experience, and then obviously reading up about what happened during the war. I think it takes a very exceptional person to be as forgiving as Steve was. Obviously, he was extremely fortunate in that his immediate parents survived. But I think most people who have gone through this sort of experience are not as forgiving as Steve is. I myself, as I say, who never experienced anything like this, find that it is very difficult to forgive the Germans for what they did. I went along to Germany with Steve as his wife. I was not keen to go. And in the 3 and 1/2 years that we had to live there, I really felt that I didn't want much contact with German people. I did try and keep myself away from any personal contact, almost living in a bubble, concentrating on my child that I had-- one child when I went, and two children when I was there-- and concentrating my whole life on the children and on Steve. And although I thought Germany was a very beautiful country and I liked the cleanliness and the discipline there, I did not want to be involved with the people in the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like me to elaborate on that, or? I think it would be really, you wanted to tell us something about Steven and his interest in Holocaust material.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One thing I do find difficult to understand now is that Steve is able to watch films on the Holocaust, watch atrocities on the screen. For instance when they showed Steve was absolutely glued from the minute it started until the end. I sat down with him initially, but found it so painful that I really could not watch. I've avoided films like which he's seen several times. I read I read I've read other books on the Holocaust. And I found by this stage of my life that it is all so painful, that I try and keep away from it. And this is, as I say, unusual because I was the person who didn't experience it. And Steve, having gone through it all, is able to watch it and is able to forgive. And for this, I admire him, because I don't think I can forgive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Hilary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the end of our interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll sit more to the left. How's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Steven, can you tell us about this family photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. Those are my parents. It's obvious that the lady on the left is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Margereta.

speaker: interviewee
text: Margereta and my father Francis, standing while she is sitting. This happened in Breslau in 1925. My mother was born and Baden, and my father was born in Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is their wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, in fact, their wedding. It's, yeah, it is their wedding photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the family wedding party. In the center, of course, we see my mother and my father. And on my mother's side and to her left are my father's parents. And to keep the balance, on my father's side and to his right are my mother's parents. People who I've mentioned before, my uncle Sam Middlebrook and his wife, who was my mother's sister, are standing in the back row, and actually above my parents. You'll be able to see them there standing at the rear. The two gentleman in front are interesting people. The one on the left in the white dress is my cousin, who recently died in New York. He survived the Holocaust. And he is the author of the book that we wrote in honor of our grandfather. The gentleman on the-- the other gentleman on the floor on the right hand side is my mother's younger brother. He served in the First World War, was wounded three times, was awarded the Iron Cross, and out of sheer gratitude, was, in the Nazi period until 1941, was forced to scrub the footpath in front of the town hall in Dusseldorf, and was then shot in the back by some Gestapo official. Most of those people on the photograph, if they were not very old-- and one or two of them died a natural death like my grandmother-- were, in fact, exterminated. If you can see the window at the rear, the right hand window there, which seems to be partially open just below it, stands my uncle, Dr. Felix Mendelsson, who, in fact, threw himself against the electrified wires in Buchenwald Concentration Camp, and thus caused his death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house of my grandfather, to which my parents, my brother and I moved during the later periods of the pre-war Nazi period, roundabout 1936-37. We lived on the ground floor and my grandfather and the housekeeper lived on the upper floor. The basement that we can't see was the one where Mr. Sacher lived, who was the housekeeper, as it were, or the maintenance man, the concierge, who, in fact, saved my grandfather, certainly, from having been taken to the concentration camp altogether. He risked his life by doing this, but nevertheless, he felt that was his duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you just like to give us the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was in Breslau. The address was then number 10, Kleinburg Strasse. And after the fall of Breslau, the house was considerably damaged. The Russians, I understand, used it as some local headquarters. And today, under Polish administration, I understand it's been converted to an old age home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my younger brother Walter and myself, taken specifically as a birthday present for my parents, and shows us in our beautiful regalia. My brother was fortunate enough to obtain a proper education at a grammar school. And that is, in fact, his school cap there. And this was, in fact, the King Edward VII School at Litchfield, to which Dr. Johnson and also Goring went. And I am in my Air Training Corp, in my cadet uniform, the Air Training Corps cadet uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you put a date to this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think this must have been 1946 or '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother Heinz, my favorite uncle, taken in Bonn, shortly before his assassination by the Nazis. He was an ardent chess player.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate given to me by the late Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, on his visit to London in 1992, when he specifically asked to meet ex-volunteers who fought with the Israeli forces in the war for liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our three children, in order of seniority, Michelle in the front, David on the right, and Daniel on the left. It was taken approximately 1988, and Michelle is about to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's about to get married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you a wedding to look forward to, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We obviously have a wedding to look forward to, and we're still looking for candidates for our two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, indeed, for sharing your memorabilia with us.
